metadata.summary: Scalable React component structure using feature-based modules, colocation, and compound component patterns with CVA variant system.

content.title: React Component Architecture
content.summary: Scalable React component structure using feature-based modules, colocation, and compound component patterns. The core rule: establish the CVA/variant pattern in one reference component (Button) before generating others — agents copy existing patterns reliably when a reference exists; barrel files must export types alongside components.

why.p: Flat `components/` folders grow unmanageable beyond ~20 files, and business logic mixed into components makes them untestable. Feature-based modules (`features/auth/`, `features/dashboard/`) colocate related components, hooks, types, and API calls so a feature can be understood and modified without touching unrelated code. Container/presenter split keeps presentational components pure and independently testable.

list.item: New React or Next.js project deciding folder structure before writing any components
Refactoring a flat `components/` folder that has grown beyond 20 files
Reviewing a component for single-responsibility violations before merging a PR
Generating a new UI primitive (Button, Input, Card) following the codebase's established variant system
Very small apps (1-3 pages, fewer than 10 components) — structure overhead exceeds benefit
Pure server-rendered applications with minimal interactivity — use Next.js server components pattern
Component libraries consumed externally — those have different export and versioning constraints

section.name: React Directory Layout and Component Folder Anatomy

rule.statement: Structure the src/ directory with: components/ui/ (base primitives), components/forms/, components/layout/, features/ (feature modules), hooks/ (shared hooks), utils/ (pure functions), types/ (shared TypeScript types), lib/ (third-party wrappers), app/ (Next.js app router or pages).
rule.rationale: The feature/ separation is the critical architectural decision. Features contain everything for a vertical slice (components, hooks, API calls, types). Shared concerns go in components/, hooks/, utils/. This boundary prevents cross-feature coupling.

rule.statement: Feature module boundaries must be strict. Components inside features/auth/ must not import from features/dashboard/. Cross-feature data flows through shared hooks, context at the app level, or a shared store — never through direct feature-to-feature imports.
rule.rationale: Cross-feature imports create hidden coupling. When features/auth/ imports from features/dashboard/, changes to dashboard can break auth. Strict boundaries make features independently modifiable.

rule.statement: Each component lives in its own folder: ComponentName.tsx (main), ComponentName.test.tsx (tests), ComponentName.stories.tsx (Storybook), index.ts (public exports). Optional: types.ts, ComponentName.module.css. No README inside component folders.
rule.rationale: Colocation: everything about a component lives in its folder. Deleting a component means deleting one folder. The index.ts barrel controls what is exported and prevents consumers from importing internal implementation details.

rule.statement: Barrel files (index.ts) must export types alongside components: export { Button } from './Button' AND export type { ButtonProps } from './Button'. Omitting type exports forces consumers to import from the internal file path.
rule.rationale: Type-only exports require explicit `export type` syntax. Agents frequently omit this, forcing consumers to import from internal paths. Once internal paths are used externally, the component folder can no longer be refactored freely.

rule.statement: UI primitives (Button, Input, Badge) should use class-variance-authority (CVA) for variant management and forwardRef for ref forwarding. Always set displayName on forwardRef components for React DevTools readability.
rule.rationale: CVA provides type-safe variant combinations with Tailwind. Without CVA, variants are managed with string concatenation that is error-prone and untyped. displayName is required for meaningful component names in DevTools; forwardRef components display as "ForwardRef" without it.

rule.statement: Use compound components (Card.Header, Card.Content, Card.Footer) when a component has multiple coordinated sub-parts. Share state between sub-parts via React Context, not prop threading.
rule.rationale: Compound components provide a clean API for complex UI blocks. Context sharing between Card.Header and Card avoids prop drilling through multiple levels while keeping the usage API declarative.

rule.statement: Container components fetch data and manage state; presenter components receive props and render UI. Container components must not contain any JSX beyond rendering the presenter and handling loading/error states. Presenter components must not contain hooks beyond useMemo/useCallback.
rule.rationale: The split makes presenters independently testable with static props and enables visual testing in Storybook without mocking data dependencies. Containers own the async lifecycle; presenters own the visual specification.

example.title: Container/Presenter Split

antipattern.description: Prop drilling: passing user through App → Layout → Sidebar → UserMenu via props when only UserMenu uses it.
antipattern.reason: Prop drilling couples every intermediate component to data it does not use. Adding or removing the prop requires touching every level. Use React Context for cross-cutting data (authentication state, theme, locale) that is needed deep in the tree.

antipattern.description: Giant components: a Dashboard component with 500 lines of JSX handling multiple concerns (stats, navigation, activity feed, sidebar).
antipattern.reason: Giant components are hard to test, review, and modify in isolation. Compose from smaller components (DashboardStats, RecentActivity, DashboardSidebar) so each part can be tested and replaced independently.

antipattern.description: Business logic mixed into components: OrderForm containing validation logic, API calls, error handling, and state updates inline in the submit handler.
antipattern.reason: Business logic in components cannot be tested without rendering. Extract to hooks (useOrderSubmission) or services. The component's job is to render UI and delegate interactions; it should not know how orders are validated or submitted.

antipattern.description: Overwriting index.ts barrel files instead of appending to them. Agents regenerate the entire index.ts rather than adding missing exports.
antipattern.reason: Overwriting a barrel file removes existing exports. Consumers of those exports get broken imports that pass TypeScript compilation (if the re-exported types still exist elsewhere) but fail at runtime. Always read the existing index.ts before writing to it.

antipattern.description: Using React Context for cross-feature communication rather than keeping it within a feature scope.
antipattern.reason: Context created in features/auth/ and consumed in features/dashboard/ re-introduces the coupling the feature boundary was designed to prevent. Cross-feature data belongs in a shared store, shared hook at the app level, or URL state.

section.name: Limitations and Failure Modes
section.p: Feature-based module structure requires discipline: agents tend to place new components in `components/` by default unless explicitly told otherwise.
Compound component pattern (Card.Header, Card.Content, etc.) breaks tree-shaking in some bundlers if sub-components are added to the root export object.
`forwardRef` boilerplate becomes verbose at scale; React 19 removes the need for it but most codebases are not yet on React 19.
Context used for cross-feature communication rather than within a feature scope re-introduces the coupling it was meant to avoid.

section.name: Agentic Workflow
section.p: An agent scaffolds a new component by reading the existing Button or Card implementation to infer the project's variant system (CVA, Tailwind tokens), then generating the new component file, test file, Storybook story, and index.ts in one pass. The agent must check for an existing `index.ts` barrel before adding exports to avoid duplicate entries. For container/presenter splits, the agent generates both files and wires them before adding route imports.
Recommended subagents: faion-sdd-executor-agent — drives sequential component creation tasks from SDD task files with quality gates.
section.code: Create a <ComponentName> component following the existing Button pattern in src/components/ui/Button/. Stack: React 18, TypeScript strict, Tailwind, CVA for variants. Variants needed: <list variants>. Include: ComponentName.tsx, ComponentName.test.tsx, ComponentName.stories.tsx, index.ts. Read Button/Button.tsx first to match token and CVA conventions.

Split <ComponentName> into a container (data/logic) and presenter (UI only). Container: <ComponentName>Container.tsx — uses hook <hookName>. Presenter: <ComponentName>.tsx — pure props, no hooks. Output both files with explicit prop interfaces.

template.purpose: Reference Button: forwardRef, CVA variants, loading state, displayName
Compound component pattern: Card with Header, Title, Content, Footer sub-components
Bash script to scaffold component folder with tsx/test/stories/index files
